expectations.evidence: All register access macros use volatile pointer casts: e.g., #define UART_SR (*(volatile uint32_t *)(UART_BASE + UART_SR_OFFSET)). All six registers (SR, DR, BRR, CR1, CR2, CR3) are accessed through volatile.
Calculates USARTDIV = 84000000 / (16 * 115200) = 45.5729. BRR mantissa=45 (0x2D), fraction=round(0.5729*16)=9, BRR=0x02D9. The compile-time integer math via USARTDIV_100 produces the same result.
Uses a 256-byte ring buffer with rx_head/rx_tail indices and power-of-2 masking: rx_tail = (rx_tail + 1U) & (RX_BUFFER_SIZE - 1U).
Uses uint8_t, uint32_t, stdbool.h (bool), stdint.h. No C++ features present. Includes stdbool.h for C99 bool type.
ISR reads UART_DR when RXNE is set: uint8_t data = (uint8_t)(UART_DR & 0xFFU). This read clears the RXNE flag.
All allocations are static: static volatile uint8_t rx_buffer[RX_BUFFER_SIZE], static volatile uint32_t rx_head/rx_tail. No malloc/calloc anywhere.
No _Static_assert or static_assert is used anywhere in the code. The comment says buffer size 'Must be a power of 2' but this is not enforced at compile time.

Documents baud error in both the code comment and Design Notes section: 'Actual baud = 84000000 / (16 * 45.5625) = 115,246 Hz, Error = (115246 - 115200) / 115200 = ~0.04%' and notes it is within +/-3% tolerance.

The response does not explicitly explain WHY reading DR clears RXNE (hardware auto-clear on read). The ISR just reads DR and there is no explanation of the auto-clear mechanism. The ORE section mentions 'reading SR then DR' clears ORE, but no equivalent explanation for RXNE.
